prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the file-integrity monitoring tool (AIDE) must have an established baseline so it can later detect unauthorized modifications to system files; without an initialized known-good database, integrity checks cannot function. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the file-integrity tool is present and that a valid baseline integrity database has been generated and installed in its active location so that subsequent integrity checks succeed.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q "^ii  aide "; then
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y aide
fi

# Initialize AIDE if not already initialized
AIDE_DB="/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz"
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

if [ ! -f "$AIDE_DB" ]; then
    sudo aideinit
    sudo mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz "$AIDE_DB"
fi

# Ensure the AIDE database is up-to-date
sudo aide --check || {
    sudo aide --init
    sudo mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz "$AIDE_DB"
}
```

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, configure the AIDE file-integrity tool so that it monitors and verifies the integrity of the system's audit tools (the binaries used to view, manipulate, and report on audit records). Each of these audit tool executables must be registered with AIDE for cryptographic verification covering their permissions, ownership, and content hashes, so that any modification, replacement, or tampering of the tools can be detected. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the audit tools are tracked by the file-integrity tool, with the configuration persisting across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the list of audit tool binaries to monitor
AUDIT_TOOLS=(
    "/sbin/auditctl"
    "/sbin/aureport"
    "/sbin/ausearch"
    "/sbin/autrace"
    "/sbin/auditd"
    "/sbin/augenrules"
    "/sbin/aulast"
    "/sbin/aulastlog"
    "/sbin/ausyscall"
    "/sbin/auvirt"
)

# Define the AIDE configuration file
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

# Define the AIDE rule for audit tools
AIDE_RULE="AuditTools"

# Function to add a rule to the AIDE configuration if it doesn't exist
add_aide_rule() {
    local rule_name="$1"
    local rule_definition="$2"
    if ! grep -q "^${rule_name} =" "$AIDE_CONF"; then
        echo "${rule_name} = ${rule_definition}" >> "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
}

# Function to add a file to the AIDE configuration if it doesn't exist
add_aide_file() {
    local file_path="$1"
    local rule_name="$2"
    if ! grep -q "^${file_path}" "$AIDE_CONF"; then
        echo "${file_path} ${rule_name}" >> "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
}

# Ensure the AIDE rule for audit tools is defined
add_aide_rule "$AIDE_RULE" "p+i+n+u+g+s+m+c+md5+sha1+sha256"

# Ensure each audit tool is tracked by AIDE
for tool in "${AUDIT_TOOLS[@]}"; do
    add_aide_file "$tool" "$AIDE_RULE"
done

# Initialize AIDE database if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f "/var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz" ]; then
    aideinit
    mv /var/lib/aide/aide.db.new.gz /var/lib/aide/aide.db.gz
fi

# Ensure AIDE is set to run at boot
if ! grep -q "aide --check" /etc/crontab; then
    echo "@reboot root /usr/bin/aide --check" >> /etc/crontab
fi
```

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the AIDE file-integrity tool is used to detect unauthorized changes to baseline system files, but undetected changes are only useful if responsible staff are alerted. Configure the system so that whenever the periodic AIDE integrity scan completes, the results of that scan are automatically delivered by email to the local root mailbox, ensuring administrators are notified of any detected modifications. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the scheduled AIDE check reports its scan details via email and that this configuration persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure mailutils is installed for sending emails
if ! dpkg -s mailutils >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y mailutils
fi

# Ensure AIDE is installed
if ! dpkg -s aide >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y aide
fi

# Create or update the cron job for AIDE
CRON_JOB="/etc/cron.daily/aide"
cat << 'EOF' > "$CRON_JOB"
#!/bin/bash

# Run AIDE check
/usr/bin/aide.wrapper --check > /tmp/aide_check_output

# Send the output to the local root mailbox
mail -s "AIDE Integrity Check Report" root < /tmp/aide_check_output

# Clean up
rm /tmp/aide_check_output
EOF

# Ensure the cron job is executable
chmod +x "$CRON_JOB"

# Ensure the mail service is enabled and started
systemctl enable postfix
systemctl start postfix
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the AIDE file-integrity tool monitors critical files for unauthorized changes, but Access Control List (ACL) attributes can grant permissions beyond the standard file mode and must also be tracked. Configure AIDE so that every file-monitoring ruleset verifies ACLs, ensuring that any ACL change on monitored files is detected during integrity checks. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures all AIDE rulesets include ACL verification.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the AIDE configuration file
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide/aide.conf"

# Backup the original configuration file
cp "$AIDE_CONF" "${AIDE_CONF}.bak"

# Function to ensure ACL is included in a rule
ensure_acl_in_rule() {
    local rule="$1"
    if [[ ! "$rule" =~ "acl" ]]; then
        rule="${rule}acl"
    fi
    echo "$rule"
}

# Process the AIDE configuration file
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the line is a rule definition
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^[A-Za-z0-9_]+[[:space:]]*=[[:space:]]*.*$ ]]; then
        # Extract the rule name and definition
        rule_name=$(echo "$line" | cut -d '=' -f 1 | xargs)
        rule_definition=$(echo "$line" | cut -d '=' -f 2- | xargs)
        
        # Ensure ACL is included in the rule definition
        new_rule_definition=$(ensure_acl_in_rule "$rule_definition")
        
        # Print the updated rule
        echo "$rule_name = $new_rule_definition"
    else
        # Print non-rule lines as is
        echo "$line"
    fi
done < "${AIDE_CONF}.bak" > "$AIDE_CONF"

# Set appropriate permissions on the AIDE configuration file
chmod 600 "$AIDE_CONF"
```

The configuration back-ends must reflect the selected policy as expected. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes this system-wide cryptographic policy and ensures it persists across reboots.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure the 'update-crypto-policies' command is available
if ! command -v update-crypto-policies &> /dev/null; then
    echo "The 'update-crypto-policies' command is not available. Please install the necessary package."

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the GnuTLS library must defer to the centralized system-wide cryptographic policy rather than overriding it with weak protocol versions. Ensure the GnuTLS back-end is configured so that it honors the system crypto policy and does not permit legacy/insecure protocol versions (SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, DTLS 0.9, DTLS 1.0), keeping cryptographic behavior consistent and centrally managed. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that brings the GnuTLS configuration into this compliant, persistent state.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the path to the GnuTLS configuration file
GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/gnutls/config"

# Define the desired configuration content
DESIRED_CONFIG="system-priority"

# Check if the GnuTLS configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    # Create the configuration file with the desired content
    echo "$DESIRED_CONFIG" > "$GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE"
else
    # Read the current configuration
    CURRENT_CONFIG=$(cat "$GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE")

    # Update the configuration if it does not match the desired state
    if [ "$CURRENT_CONFIG" != "$DESIRED_CONFIG" ]; then
        echo "$DESIRED_CONFIG" > "$GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
fi

# Ensure the correct permissions are set on the configuration file
chmod 644 "$GNUTLS_CONFIG_FILE"

echo "GnuTLS configuration has been updated to defer to the system-wide crypto policy."
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, ensure the SSH server obtains its cipher selection from the centralized system-wide cryptographic policy rather than from a local override, so that only FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers (ordered strongest-to-weakest) are used and the configuration remains consistent and non-fragmented. The relevant crypto-policy back-end for the SSH server must contain the correct cipher specification, uncommented and aligned with the system policy. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces this end state and persists across reboots.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the system-wide crypto policy is set to FIPS
update-crypto-policies --set FIPS

# Ensure SSHD uses the system-wide crypto policy
SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
SSHD_INCLUDE_LINE="Include /etc/crypto-policies/back-ends/openssh.config"

# Check if the Include line is already present and correct
if ! grep -q "^${SSHD_INCLUDE_LINE}$" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    # Remove any existing Include lines for openssh.config
    sed -i '/^Include \/etc\/crypto-policies\/back-ends\/openssh.config$/d' "$SSHD_CONFIG"
    # Add the correct Include line at the end of the file
    echo "$SSHD_INCLUDE_LINE" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

# Restart the SSHD service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system using the default GNOME graphical desktop environment, configure the desktop so that removing a user's smartcard immediately locks the screen, preventing unauthorized access to an unattended session when a user steps away with their card. The setting must be enforced as a mandatory system-wide policy that ordinary users cannot override, and it must persist across reboots. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes this behavior.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the required package is installed
apt-get update
apt-get install -y libpam-pkcs11

# Create the configuration directory if it doesn't exist
mkdir -p /etc/pam_pkcs11

# Configure PAM to lock the screen on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/pam_pkcs11/pam_pkcs11.conf
use_pkcs11_module = opensc {
    module = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/opensc-pkcs11.so;
    description = "OpenSC PKCS#11 module";
};

use_mappers = digest;

digest {
    module = internal;
    func = digest;
    params = "sha1";
};

EOF

# Configure the PAM service to lock the screen on smartcard removal
cat << 'EOF' > /etc/pam.d/common-auth
auth    [success=2 default=ignore]      pam_pkcs11.so
auth    [success=1 default=ignore]      pam_unix.so nullok_secure
auth    requisite                       pam_deny.so
auth    required                        pam_permit.so
auth    optional                        pam_cap.so
EOF

To minimize the window in which an unattended or hijacked session could perform unauthorized privileged operations, configure sudo so that its credential-caching interval requires re-authentication on an appropriately short, non-indefinite schedule (the cached authentication must expire and must never be set to never expire). response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the desired timeout in minutes
TIMEOUT=5

# Define the sudoers file to modify
SUDOERS_FILE="/etc/sudoers"

# Create a temporary file for the new sudoers configuration
TEMP_FILE=$(mktemp)

# Ensure the temporary file is removed on exit
trap "rm -f $TEMP_FILE" EXIT

# Copy the current sudoers file to the temporary file
cp "$SUDOERS_FILE" "$TEMP_FILE"

# Check if the timestamp_timeout setting is already present
if grep -q "^Defaults.*timestamp_timeout=" "$SUDOERS_FILE"; then
    # Update the existing timestamp_timeout setting
    sed -i "s/^Defaults.*timestamp_timeout=.*/Defaults\ttimestamp_timeout=$TIMEOUT/" "$TEMP_FILE"
else
    # Add the timestamp_timeout setting
    echo "Defaults    timestamp_timeout=$TIMEOUT" >> "$TEMP_FILE"
fi

# Validate the new sudoers configuration
visudo -c -f "$TEMP_FILE"
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    # If valid, move the temporary file to the sudoers file
    mv "$TEMP_FILE" "$SUDOERS_FILE"
else
    echo "Error: Invalid sudoers configuration. Changes not applied."
    exit 1
fi
```

# Enforce GPG signature verification for local package installations

EOF

echo "Signature verification for local package installations is enforced."

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the authselect tool is installed
if ! command -v authselect &> /dev/null; then
    echo "authselect is not installed. Installing..."
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y authselect
fi

# Define the desired profile
DESIRED_PROFILE="sssd"

# Check the current profile
CURRENT_PROFILE=$(authselect current | grep "Profile ID" | awk '{print $3}')

# Apply the desired profile if it's not already active
if [ "$CURRENT_PROFILE" != "$DESIRED_PROFILE" ]; then
    echo "Switching to the $DESIRED_PROFILE profile..."
    sudo authselect select $DESIRED_PROFILE with-sudo --force
else
    echo "The $DESIRED_PROFILE profile is already active."
fi

# Ensure changes persist across reboots
sudo authselect apply-changes
```

The banner display must be enabled and locked so that ordinary users cannot disable or override it, and the configuration must persist across reboots. response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the banner text
BANNER_TEXT="Authorized users only. Unauthorized access is prohibited."

# Define the path to the GDM configuration file
GDM_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/00-login-banner"

# Define the lock file path
LOCK_FILE="/etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks/login-banner"

# Ensure the dconf directory exists
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d
mkdir -p /etc/dconf/db/gdm.d/locks

# Create or update the GDM configuration file with the banner text
cat <<EOF > "$GDM_CONFIG_FILE"

EOF

# Create or update the lock file to prevent users from changing the settings
echo "/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-enable" > "$LOCK_FILE"
echo "/org/gnome/login-screen/banner-message-text" >> "$LOCK_FILE"

# Update the dconf database
dconf update

# Ensure the gdm service is restarted to apply changes
systemctl restart gdm
```

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the PAM configuration file for common authentication
PAM_AUTH_FILE="/etc/pam.d/common-auth"

# Define the PAM configuration file for common account
PAM_ACCOUNT_FILE="/etc/pam.d/common-account"

# Define the lockout parameters
FAIL_INTERVAL=900
DENY=5
UNLOCK_TIME=600

# Function to ensure a line is present in a file
ensure_line_in_file() {
    local file="$1"
    local line="$2"
    grep -qF -- "$line" "$file" || echo "$line" >> "$file"
}

# Ensure pam_tally2 is configured in the auth section
ensure_line_in_file "$PAM_AUTH_FILE" "auth required pam_tally2.so deny=$DENY unlock_time=$UNLOCK_TIME even_deny_root"

# Ensure pam_tally2 is configured in the account section
ensure_line_in_file "$PAM_ACCOUNT_FILE" "account required pam_tally2.so"

# Ensure pam_tally2 is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q pam-tally2; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y libpam-modules
fi

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the audit service is installed and enabled
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q auditd; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y auditd
fi

systemctl enable auditd
systemctl start auditd

# Define the audit rule for account lockout
audit_rule="-w /var/log/auth.log -p wa -k account-lockout"

# Check if the audit rule is already present
if ! auditctl -l | grep -q "$audit_rule"; then
    echo "$audit_rule" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/account-lockout.rules
fi

# Ensure the audit rules are loaded
augenrules --load

# Ensure pam_tally2 or pam_faillock is configured for account lockout
pam_file="/etc/pam.d/common-auth"
if ! grep -q "pam_tally2.so" "$pam_file" && ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$pam_file"; then
    echo "auth required pam_tally2.so deny=5 unlock_time=900 onerr=fail audit" >> "$pam_file"
fi

# Reload the PAM configuration
pam-auth-update --force

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Install necessary package
apt-get update
apt-get install -y libpam-tally2

# Define the PAM configuration files to modify
PAM_FILES=(
    "/etc/pam.d/common-auth"
    "/etc/pam.d/common-account"
)

# Function to configure PAM for persistent lockout
configure_pam() {
    local file="$1"
    local auth_line="auth required pam_tally2.so deny=5 unlock_time=900 onerr=fail audit file=/var/log/tallylog"
    local account_line="account required pam_tally2.so"

    else
        echo "$auth_line" >> "$file"

        sed -i "/pam_tally2.so/c\\$account_line" "$file"
    else
        echo "$account_line" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Apply configuration to each PAM file
for file in "${PAM_FILES[@]}"; do
    configure_pam "$file"
done

# Ensure the tallylog file exists and has the correct permissions
touch /var/log/tallylog
chmod 600 /var/log/tallylog
chown root:root /var/log/tallylog

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define variables for lockout policy
LOCKOUT_DENY=5
LOCKOUT_TIME=900
LOCKOUT_FAIL_INTERVAL=900

# Ensure the necessary packages are installed
apt-get update
apt-get install -y libpam-pwquality

# Configure pam_tally2 for account lockout
PAM_TALLY2_CONF="/etc/pam.d/common-auth"
if ! grep -q "pam_tally2.so" "$PAM_TALLY2_CONF"; then
    echo "auth required pam_tally2.so deny=$LOCKOUT_DENY unlock_time=$LOCKOUT_TIME even_deny_root root_unlock_time=$LOCKOUT_TIME" >> "$PAM_TALLY2_CONF"
else
    sed -i "s/auth required pam_tally2.so.*/auth required pam_tally2.so deny=$LOCKOUT_DENY unlock_time=$LOCKOUT_TIME even_deny_root root_unlock_time=$LOCKOUT_TIME/" "$PAM_TALLY2_CONF"
fi

# Configure pam_faillock for account lockout
PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF="/etc/security/faillock.conf"
if [ ! -f "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF" ]; then
    touch "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"
fi

sed -i "/^deny/d" "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"
sed -i "/^unlock_time/d" "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"
sed -i "/^fail_interval/d" "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"

echo "deny = $LOCKOUT_DENY" >> "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"
echo "unlock_time = $LOCKOUT_TIME" >> "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"
echo "fail_interval = $LOCKOUT_FAIL_INTERVAL" >> "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"

# Ensure pam_faillock is configured in the PAM stack
PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH="/etc/pam.d/common-auth"
if ! grep -q "pam_faillock.so" "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"; then
    sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/i auth required pam_faillock.so preauth silent audit deny='"$LOCKOUT_DENY"' unlock_time='"$LOCKOUT_TIME"' fail_interval='"$LOCKOUT_FAIL_INTERVAL" "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"
    sed -i '/^auth.*required.*pam_deny.so/i auth [default=die] pam_faillock.so authfail audit deny='"$LOCKOUT_DENY"' unlock_time='"$LOCKOUT_TIME"' fail_interval='"$LOCKOUT_FAIL_INTERVAL" "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"
    sed -i '/^account.*required.*pam_unix.so/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"
fi

# Update PAM configuration to ensure changes are applied
pam-auth-update --force

sed -i "/^fail_interval/d" "$PAM_FAILLOCK_CONF"

    sed -i '/^account.*required.*pam_unix.so/i account required pam_faillock.so' "$PAM_SYSTEM_AUTH"
fi

This makes newly chosen passwords less likely to resemble previously compromised ones and increases resistance to guessing. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces this minimum-different-characters requirement for password changes system-wide, persisting across reboots.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the pam_python module is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q "libpam-python"; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y libpam-python
fi

# Create the Python script for the PAM module
cat << 'EOF' > /usr/local/lib/security/pam_min_diff.py
def pam_sm_authenticate(pamh, flags, argv):
    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS

def pam_sm_setcred(pamh, flags, argv):
    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS

def pam_sm_acct_mgmt(pamh, flags, argv):
    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS

def pam_sm_open_session(pamh, flags, argv):
    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS

def pam_sm_close_session(pamh, flags, argv):
    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS

def pam_sm_chauthtok(pamh, flags, argv):
    if flags & pamh.PAM_PRELIM_CHECK:
        return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS
    if flags & pamh.PAM_UPDATE_AUTHTOK:
        old_password = pamh.oldauthtok
        new_password = pamh.authtok
        if old_password is None or new_password is None:
            return pamh.PAM_AUTHTOK_ERR

        old_set = set(old_password)
        new_set = set(new_password)
        diff_count = len(new_set - old_set)

        if diff_count < 8:
            return pamh.PAM_AUTHTOK_ERR

    return pamh.PAM_SUCCESS
EOF

# Ensure the Python script is executable
chmod 755 /usr/local/lib/security/pam_min_diff.py

# Add the PAM module to the password stack
if ! grep -q "pam_python.so /usr/local/lib/security/pam_min_diff.py" /etc/pam.d/common-password; then
    echo "password requisite pam_python.so /usr/local/lib/security/pam_min_diff.py" >> /etc/pam.d/common-password
fi
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the minimum password length
MIN_LENGTH=15

# Function to update or add a configuration in a file
update_config() {
    local file="$1"
    local key="$2"
    local value="$3"
    local separator="$4"
    local comment_char="$5"

    if grep -qE "^\s*${key}\s*${separator}" "$file"; then
        # Update existing configuration
        sed -i "s/^\s*${key}\s*${separator}.*/${key}${separator}${value}/" "$file"
    else
        # Add new configuration
        echo "${key}${separator}${value}" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Update /etc/security/pwquality.conf
update_config "/etc/security/pwquality.conf" "minlen" "$MIN_LENGTH" "=" "#"

# Update /etc/pam.d/common-password
if ! grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/common-password; then
    echo "password requisite pam_pwquality.so" >> /etc/pam.d/common-password
fi

if grep -q "pam_pwquality.so" /etc/pam.d/common-password; then
    sed -i "/pam_pwquality.so/ s/\(minlen=\)[0-9]*/\1$MIN_LENGTH/" /etc/pam.d/common-password
else
    echo "password requisite pam_pwquality.so minlen=$MIN_LENGTH" >> /etc/pam.d/common-password
fi

#!/bin/bash

    echo "Error: $LOGIN_DEFS_FILE does not exist."

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Set the maximum password age
MAX_PASS_AGE=365

# Get all local user accounts with a UID greater than or equal to 1000
# Exclude system accounts with UIDs less than 1000
for user in $(awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 {print $1}' /etc/passwd); do
    # Get the current maximum password age for the user
    current_max_age=$(chage -l "$user" | grep "Maximum number of days between password change" | awk '{print $NF}')
    
    # If the current maximum age is greater than the desired maximum age or is set to 'never', update it
    if [ "$current_max_age" -gt "$MAX_PASS_AGE" ] || [ "$current_max_age" == "never" ]; then
        chage --maxdays $MAX_PASS_AGE "$user"
    fi
done

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, ensure that every file and directory within each local interactive user's home directory is group-owned by a group of which that user is a member, so that files cannot be inadvertently exposed to unrelated users through an inappropriate group ownership. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that corrects the group ownership of all such files and directories for all local interactive users.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Get a list of all local interactive users
getent passwd | awk -F: '$3 >= 1000 && $3 < 65534 {print $1}' | while read -r user; do
    # Get the user's home directory
    home_dir=$(getent passwd "$user" | cut -d: -f6)
    
    # Get the user's primary group
    primary_group=$(id -gn "$user")
    
    # Ensure the home directory exists
    if [ -d "$home_dir" ]; then
        # Find all files and directories in the user's home directory
        find "$home_dir" -exec chgrp "$primary_group" {} +
    fi
done

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Function to adjust permissions
adjust_permissions() {
    local file="$1"
    local current_perms
    current_perms=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")

    # Only change permissions if they are more permissive than 0740
    if (( current_perms > 740 )); then
        chmod 740 "$file"
    fi
}

# Get a list of all human users with home directories
users=$(awk -F: '($3 >= 1000 || $1 == "root") && $7 ~ /(bash|sh|zsh|ksh)$/ {print $1}' /etc/passwd)

# Iterate over each user
for user in $users; do
    home_dir=$(eval echo "~$user")
    
    # Check if home directory exists
    if [ -d "$home_dir" ]; then
        # List of common shell initialization files
        files=(".bashrc" ".bash_profile" ".profile" ".zshrc" ".kshrc" ".login" ".cshrc")

        for file in "${files[@]}"; do
            full_path="$home_dir/$file"
            if [ -f "$full_path" ]; then
                adjust_permissions "$full_path"
            fi
        done
    fi
done

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation to mitigate the Meltdown vulnerability and strengthen defenses against attempts to defeat kernel address-space layout randomization. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that turns on this kernel hardening feature via the bootloader so that it takes effect on boot, applies to all currently installed kernels, and is automatically carried over to any newly installed kernels, persisting across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the GRUB configuration file
GRUB_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/default/grub"

# Define the kernel parameter to enable Kernel Page-Table Isolation
KPTI_PARAM="pti=on"

# Function to update GRUB configuration
update_grub_config() {
    if grep -q "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"; then
        if grep -q "$KPTI_PARAM" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"; then
            echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation is already enabled."
        else
            sed -i "/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=/ s/\"$/ $KPTI_PARAM\"/" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"
            echo "Kernel Page-Table Isolation parameter added."
        fi
    else
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"quiet splash $KPTI_PARAM\"" >> "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT entry created with Kernel Page-Table Isolation parameter."
    fi
}

# Function to update GRUB
update_grub() {
    if command -v update-grub &> /dev/null; then
        update-grub
    elif command -v grub-mkconfig &> /dev/null && command -v grub-install &> /dev/null; then
        grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg
    else
        echo "Error: GRUB update command not found."
        exit 1
    fi
}

# Main execution
update_grub_config
update_grub

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the system logging service forwards audit and log records to a remote collector. To prevent records from being sent to an impersonated or unauthorized destination, the logging service must be configured to authenticate the remote peer using certificate-based (x.509) verification of the peer's name before off-loading any records over the encrypted connection. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces certificate-name authentication of the remote log destination, ensuring the setting persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Variables
REMOTE_LOG_HOST="remote.log.server"
REMOTE_LOG_PORT="6514"
CERTIFICATE_FILE="/etc/ssl/certs/remote-log-server-cert.pem"
SYSLOG_CONF="/etc/rsyslog.d/50-default.conf"
RSYSLOG_TLS_CONF="/etc/rsyslog.d/01-tls.conf"

# Ensure the certificate file exists
if [ ! -f "$CERTIFICATE_FILE" ]; then
    echo "Certificate file $CERTIFICATE_FILE not found. Exiting."
    exit 1
fi

# Create or update the rsyslog TLS configuration
cat <<EOF > "$RSYSLOG_TLS_CONF"
# Rsyslog TLS configuration
\$DefaultNetstreamDriverCAFile $CERTIFICATE_FILE
\$ActionSendStreamDriver gtls
\$ActionSendStreamDriverMode 1
\$ActionSendStreamDriverAuthMode x509/name
\$ActionSendStreamDriverPermittedPeer $REMOTE_LOG_HOST
EOF

# Ensure the remote log destination is configured
if ! grep -q "@@$REMOTE_LOG_HOST:$REMOTE_LOG_PORT" "$SYSLOG_CONF"; then
    echo "*.* @@$REMOTE_LOG_HOST:$REMOTE_LOG_PORT" >> "$SYSLOG_CONF"
fi

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

# Enable rsyslog to start on boot
systemctl enable rsyslog
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Function to disable ICMP redirects for all IPv6 interfaces
disable_icmp_redirects() {
    local sysctl_conf="/etc/sysctl.conf"
    local setting="net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects=0"
    local runtime_path="/proc/sys/net/ipv6/conf/all/accept_redirects"

    # Apply the change at runtime
    echo 0 > "$runtime_path"

    # Ensure the setting is present in sysctl.conf
    if grep -q "^$setting" "$sysctl_conf"; then
        sed -i "s/^$setting.*/$setting/" "$sysctl_conf"
    else
        echo "$setting" >> "$sysctl_conf"
    fi

    # Reload sysctl settings to ensure persistence
    sysctl -p > /dev/null
}

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Function to apply the sysctl setting
apply_sysctl_setting() {
    local key="$1"
    local value="$2"
    local file="/etc/sysctl.conf"

    # Apply the setting immediately
    sysctl -w "$key=$value"

    # Ensure the setting is present in /etc/sysctl.conf
    if grep -q "^$key" "$file"; then
        sed -i "s|^$key.*|$key = $value|" "$file"
    else
        echo "$key = $value" >> "$file"
    fi
}

# Apply the setting for ignoring ICMP echo requests to broadcast/multicast
apply_sysctl_setting "net.ipv4.icmp_echo_ignore_broadcasts" "1"

# Reload sysctl settings to ensure persistence
sysctl -p
```

# Create a configuration file to blacklist the module

if [ ! -f "$BLACKLIST_CONF" ] || ! grep -q "^blacklist $MODULE_NAME" "$BLACKLIST_CONF"; then

fi

fi

# Remove the module if it is currently loaded
if lsmod | grep -q "^$MODULE_NAME"; then
    sudo rmmod "$MODULE_NAME"
fi

if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root."
    exit 1

# Create or update the blacklist configuration for CAN protocol
BLACKLIST_CONF="/etc/modprobe.d/blacklist-can.conf"

echo -e "blacklist can\nblacklist can_raw\nblacklist can_bcm\nblacklist can_gw" > "$BLACKLIST_CONF"

fi

# Update GRUB configuration
update-grub

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Function to update /etc/fstab
update_fstab() {
    local boot_uuid="$1"
    local boot_options="$2"
    local fstab_entry

    # Check if there's already an entry for the boot partition
    if grep -q "UUID=${boot_uuid}" /etc/fstab; then
        # Update the existing entry
        sed -i "s|\(UUID=${boot_uuid}.*\)defaults\(.*\)|\1defaults,${boot_options}\2|" /etc/fstab
    else
        # Add a new entry for the boot partition
        echo "UUID=${boot_uuid} /boot ext4 defaults,${boot_options} 0 2" >> /etc/fstab
    fi
}

# Function to remount the boot partition with nosuid
remount_boot() {
    local boot_mount_point="$1"
    mount -o remount,nosuid "${boot_mount_point}"
}

# Get the boot partition mount point and UUID
boot_mount_point=$(findmnt -n -o TARGET /boot)
boot_uuid=$(blkid -s UUID -o value "$(findmnt -n -o SOURCE /boot)")

# Check if /boot is a separate partition
if [ -n "$boot_mount_point" ] && [ "$boot_mount_point" = "/boot" ]; then
    # Remount /boot with nosuid option
    remount_boot "$boot_mount_point"

    # Update /etc/fstab to persist the change
    update_fstab "$boot_uuid" "nosuid"
fi
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, ensure that every non-root local filesystem is mounted in a way that prevents any files on it from being interpreted as character or block device nodes, since legitimate device files should exist only under the root partition's device directory (or within purpose-built chroot jails). Permitting device-node interpretation on other partitions could let an attacker plant rogue device files to gain unauthorized access to underlying hardware or memory. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that applies this protection to all non-root local partitions so that it persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Get a list of all non-root local filesystems
non_root_filesystems=$(lsblk -lnpo NAME,MOUNTPOINT,FSTYPE | awk '$2 && $2 != "/" && $3 != "swap" {print $1}')

# Function to update /etc/fstab to include nodev option
update_fstab() {
    local device=$1
    local mountpoint=$2
    local fstype=$3

    # Check if the entry already has nodev option
    if ! grep -q "^[^#]*${device}.*${mountpoint}.*nodev" /etc/fstab; then
        # Add nodev option to the mount entry
        sed -i "s|^\([^#]*${device}.*${mountpoint}.*${fstype}.*\)|\1,nodev|" /etc/fstab
    fi
}

# Iterate over each non-root filesystem
while read -r line; do
    device=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $1}')
    mountpoint=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $2}')
    fstype=$(echo "$line" | awk '{print $3}')

    # Ensure the filesystem is mounted with nodev option
    mount -o remount,nodev "$mountpoint"

    # Update /etc/fstab to persist the nodev option
    update_fstab "$device" "$mountpoint" "$fstype"
done <<< "$non_root_filesystems"
```

#!/bin/bash

# Function to apply the ptrace scope restriction
apply_ptrace_restriction() {
    local ptrace_scope_file="/proc/sys/kernel/yama/ptrace_scope"
    local sysctl_conf_file="/etc/sysctl.d/10-ptrace.conf"

    # Check if the ptrace_scope file exists
    if [ -f "$ptrace_scope_file" ]; then
        # Set the ptrace_scope to 1 for current session
        echo 1 > "$ptrace_scope_file"
    fi

    # Ensure the setting persists across reboots
    echo "kernel.yama.ptrace_scope = 1" > "$sysctl_conf_file"

    # Apply the sysctl settings
    sysctl --system > /dev/null
}

```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
    echo "This script must be run as root" >&2
    exit 1
fi

# Update package lists
apt-get update

# Install AppArmor if not already installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q apparmor; then
    apt-get install -y apparmor apparmor-utils
fi

# Enable AppArmor service
systemctl enable apparmor
systemctl start apparmor

# Create a default deny-all policy
DEFAULT_PROFILE="/etc/apparmor.d/usr.bin.denyall"
if [ ! -f "$DEFAULT_PROFILE" ]; then
    cat <<EOF > "$DEFAULT_PROFILE"
#include <tunables/global>

/usr/bin/* {
  # Deny all execution by default
  deny /usr/bin/* mr,
}
EOF
    apparmor_parser -r "$DEFAULT_PROFILE"
fi

# Ensure the deny-all profile is loaded
aa-enforce usr.bin.denyall

# Reload AppArmor profiles
systemctl reload apparmor

echo "AppArmor deny-all policy is now enforced."
```

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure chrony is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q chrony; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y chrony
fi

# Backup the original configuration file
CHRONY_CONF="/etc/chrony/chrony.conf"
if [ ! -f "${CHRONY_CONF}.bak" ]; then
    cp "${CHRONY_CONF}" "${CHRONY_CONF}.bak"
fi

# Configure chrony to operate in client-only mode
sed -i '/^server /!b;n;c\iburst' "${CHRONY_CONF}"
sed -i '/^pool /!b;n;c\iburst' "${CHRONY_CONF}"
sed -i '/^allow /d' "${CHRONY_CONF}"
sed -i '/^bindaddress /d' "${CHRONY_CONF}"
sed -i '/^bindcmdaddress /d' "${CHRONY_CONF}"

# Restart chrony service to apply changes
systemctl restart chrony

# Enable chrony service to start on boot
systemctl enable chrony
```

Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures each remote time source used by the system's time daemon has an explicit maximum polling interval defined, with this setting persisting across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the script is run as root
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
  echo "This script must be run as root."
  exit 1
fi

# Define the maximum polling interval
MAX_POLL_INTERVAL=10

# Install chrony if not already installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q chrony; then
  apt-get update
  apt-get install -y chrony
fi

# Backup the original chrony configuration file
CHRONY_CONF="/etc/chrony/chrony.conf"
if [ ! -f "${CHRONY_CONF}.bak" ]; then
  cp "${CHRONY_CONF}" "${CHRONY_CONF}.bak"
fi

# Function to update or add maxpoll to server lines
update_maxpoll() {
  local conf_file="$1"
  local max_poll="$2"
  local temp_file=$(mktemp)

  while IFS= read -r line; do
    if [[ "$line" =~ ^server\  ]]; then
      if [[ "$line" =~ maxpoll ]]; then
        echo "$line" | sed -E "s/(maxpoll[[:space:]]+)[0-9]+/\1${max_poll}/" >> "$temp_file"
      else
        echo "$line maxpoll ${max_poll}" >> "$temp_file"
      fi
    else
      echo "$line" >> "$temp_file"
    fi
  done < "$conf_file"

  mv "$temp_file" "$conf_file"
}

# Update the chrony configuration
update_maxpoll "$CHRONY_CONF" "$MAX_POLL_INTERVAL"

# Restart the chrony service to apply changes
systemctl restart chrony

# Enable chrony service to start on boot
systemctl enable chrony
```

SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

    if grep -qE "^\s*${key}\s+" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, configure the SSH server so that remote logins to accounts that have empty passwords are explicitly forbidden. Even if an account is misconfigured with a blank password, the SSH service must refuse such authentication, closing a high-risk avenue for unauthorized remote access. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces this restriction for the SSH daemon, ensuring the setting persists across reboots and service restarts.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the SSH configuration file path
SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

# Define the setting to be enforced
SETTING="PermitEmptyPasswords no"

# Ensure the SSH configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "SSH configuration file not found: $SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original SSH configuration file
cp "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE" "${SSH_CONFIG_FILE}.bak"

# Check if the setting is already present and correct
if grep -qE "^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords\s+no\s*$" "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"; then
    echo "The setting is already correctly configured."
else
    # Remove any existing PermitEmptyPasswords lines
    sed -i '/^\s*PermitEmptyPasswords\s\+/d' "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    
    # Add the correct setting
    echo "$SETTING" >> "$SSH_CONFIG_FILE"
    
    echo "The setting has been added to the SSH configuration."
fi

# Restart the SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

# Verify the SSH service is running
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    echo "SSH service is running with the updated configuration."
else
    echo "Failed to restart the SSH service. Please check the configuration."
    exit 1
fi
```

SSH_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

# Define the setting to disable user environment processing

fi

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system using the system security services daemon (SSSD) for smart-card / certificate-based multifactor authentication, configure it so that the validity of authentication certificates is verified in real time against the issuing authority's revocation status using the Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP). This prevents revoked or compromised certificates from being accepted for login. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enables OCSP-based certificate status checking in SSSD and ensures the setting persists.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the SSSD configuration file
SSSD_CONF="/etc/sssd/sssd.conf"

# Ensure the configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$SSSD_CONF" ]; then
    echo "SSSD configuration file not found at $SSSD_CONF"
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original configuration file
cp "$SSSD_CONF" "${SSSD_CONF}.bak"

# Function to set or update a configuration option
set_config_option() {
    local section="$1"
    local option="$2"
    local value="$3"
    local config_file="$4"

    # Check if the section exists
    if ! grep -q "^\[$section\]" "$config_file"; then
        # Add the section if it doesn't exist
        echo -e "\n[$section]" >> "$config_file"
    fi

    # Check if the option exists in the section
    if grep -q "^\[$section\]" -A 100 "$config_file" | grep -q "^$option"; then
        # Update the existing option
        sed -i "/^\[$section\]/,/^\[/{s/^$option.*/$option = $value/}" "$config_file"
    else
        # Add the option to the section
        sed -i "/^\[$section\]/a $option = $value" "$config_file"
    fi
}

# Set the OCSP options in the [pam] section
set_config_option "pam" "pam_cert_auth" "True" "$SSSD_CONF"
set_config_option "pam" "pam_cert_ocsp" "True" "$SSSD_CONF"

# Restart the SSSD service to apply changes
systemctl restart sssd

# Check the status of the SSSD service
if systemctl is-active --quiet sssd; then
    echo "SSSD service restarted successfully."
else
    echo "Failed to restart SSSD service."
    exit 1
fi
```

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, configure the system security services daemon to support smart-card (e.g. CAC/PIV token) based authentication, so that users can authenticate using a separate hardware credential rather than relying solely on passwords — providing multi-factor assurance even if the host itself is compromised. The end state must enable smart-card authentication within the system services daemon and integrate it into the host's authentication stack so that certificate-based login via the daemon is honored during authentication. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes this configuration and persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

set -e

# Ensure necessary packages are installed
apt-get update
apt-get install -y opensc pcscd libpam-pkcs11

# Enable and start the pcscd service
systemctl enable pcscd
systemctl start pcscd

# Configure PAM for smart card authentication
PAM_PKCS11_CONF="/etc/pam_pkcs11/pam_pkcs11.conf"
if [ ! -f "$PAM_PKCS11_CONF" ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/pam_pkcs11
    cat <<EOF > "$PAM_PKCS11_CONF"
use_pkcs11_module = opensc;
pkcs11_module opensc {
    module = /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/opensc-pkcs11.so;
    description = "OpenSC PKCS#11 module";
};
EOF
fi

# Configure NSS database for system-wide use
NSSDB_DIR="/etc/pki/nssdb"
if [ ! -d "$NSSDB_DIR" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$NSSDB_DIR"
    certutil -N -d "$NSSDB_DIR" --empty-password
fi

# Update PAM configuration for smart card authentication
PAM_SSHD_CONF="/etc/pam.d/sshd"
if ! grep -q "pam_pkcs11.so" "$PAM_SSHD_CONF"; then
    echo "auth    required    pam_pkcs11.so" >> "$PAM_SSHD_CONF"
fi

# Update SSH configuration to support smart card authentication
SSHD_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"
if ! grep -q "^UsePAM yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    echo "UsePAM yes" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

if ! grep -q "^PubkeyAuthentication yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    echo "PubkeyAuthentication yes" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

if ! grep -q "^SmartcardAuthentication yes" "$SSHD_CONFIG"; then
    echo "SmartcardAuthentication yes" >> "$SSHD_CONFIG"
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

echo "Smart card authentication configuration completed successfully."
```

Write an idempotent bash remediation script that generates an initial device-authorization policy reflecting the USB devices currently connected to the system, so that legitimate existing devices remain permitted, and ensure the resulting policy is in place and persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure USBGuard is installed
if ! command -v usbguard &> /dev/null; then
    echo "USBGuard is not installed. Installing..."
    sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y usbguard
fi

# Start USBGuard service if not running
if ! systemctl is-active --quiet usbguard; then
    echo "Starting USBGuard service..."
    sudo systemctl start usbguard
    sudo systemctl enable usbguard
fi

# Generate initial policy based on currently connected devices
echo "Generating initial USBGuard policy..."
sudo usbguard generate-policy > /tmp/initial-usbguard-policy.conf

# Backup existing policy if it exists
if [ -f /etc/usbguard/rules.conf ]; then
    echo "Backing up existing USBGuard policy..."
    sudo cp /etc/usbguard/rules.conf /etc/usbguard/rules.conf.bak
fi

# Apply the generated policy
echo "Applying the new USBGuard policy..."
sudo cp /tmp/initial-usbguard-policy.conf /etc/usbguard/rules.conf

# Ensure the policy is loaded
echo "Reloading USBGuard rules..."
sudo usbguard reload-rules

# Clean up temporary files
rm /tmp/initial-usbguard-policy.conf

echo "USBGuard policy has been successfully updated and applied."
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit kernel parameter
AUDIT_PARAM="audit=1"

# Function to update GRUB configuration
update_grub_config() {
    local grub_file="/etc/default/grub"
    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT" "$grub_file"; then
        if grep -q "$AUDIT_PARAM" "$grub_file"; then
            echo "Audit parameter already set in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT."
        else
            sed -i "s/^\(GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\".*\)\"/\1 $AUDIT_PARAM\"/" "$grub_file"
            echo "Added audit parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT."
        fi
    else
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT=\"$AUDIT_PARAM\"" >> "$grub_file"
        echo "Created GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT with audit parameter."
    fi

    if grep -q "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX" "$grub_file"; then
        if grep -q "$AUDIT_PARAM" "$grub_file"; then
            echo "Audit parameter already set in GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX."
        else
            sed -i "s/^\(GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\".*\)\"/\1 $AUDIT_PARAM\"/" "$grub_file"
            echo "Added audit parameter to GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX."
        fi
    else
        echo "GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX=\"$AUDIT_PARAM\"" >> "$grub_file"
        echo "Created GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX with audit parameter."
    fi

    update-grub
}

# Function to update kernel command line for future kernels
update_kernel_cmdline() {
    local kernel_cmdline_file="/etc/kernel/cmdline"
    if [ -f "$kernel_cmdline_file" ]; then
        if grep -q "$AUDIT_PARAM" "$kernel_cmdline_file"; then
            echo "Audit parameter already set in kernel cmdline."
        else
            echo "$AUDIT_PARAM" >> "$kernel_cmdline_file"
            echo "Added audit parameter to kernel cmdline."
        fi
    else
        echo "$AUDIT_PARAM" > "$kernel_cmdline_file"
        echo "Created kernel cmdline with audit parameter."
    fi
}

# Apply the changes
update_grub_config
update_kernel_cmdline
```

On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the kernel must be able to queue audit events generated during early boot before the audit daemon starts; if this queue is too small it can overflow and trigger the audit failure action, causing loss of audit records. Configure the system so that the boot-time audit event queue holds at least 8192 entries, ensuring this setting applies to all currently installed kernels and to any kernels installed in the future, and persists across reboots. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that achieves this.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the required audit queue size
REQUIRED_AUDIT_QUEUE_SIZE=8192

# Define the configuration file for GRUB
GRUB_CONFIG_FILE="/etc/default/grub"

# Function to update GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT
update_grub_cmdline() {
    local current_cmdline
    current_cmdline=$(grep "^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE")

    if [[ $current_cmdline == *"audit_backlog_limit="* ]]; then
        # Update existing audit_backlog_limit value
        sed -i "s/audit_backlog_limit=[0-9]*/audit_backlog_limit=$REQUIRED_AUDIT_QUEUE_SIZE/" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"
    else
        # Append audit_backlog_limit to the existing GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT
        sed -i "/^GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT/s/\"$/ audit_backlog_limit=$REQUIRED_AUDIT_QUEUE_SIZE\"/" "$GRUB_CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
}

# Update the GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT
update_grub_cmdline

# Update GRUB configuration
update-grub

# Inform the user
echo "Audit backlog limit set to $REQUIRED_AUDIT_QUEUE_SIZE and GRUB configuration updated."
```

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the auditd service is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q auditd; then
    apt-get update && apt-get install -y auditd
fi

# Create the audit rules directory if it doesn't exist
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Define the immutable rule
IMMUTABLE_RULE="-e 2"

# Check if the immutable rule is already set
if ! grep -q "$IMMUTABLE_RULE" "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"/immutable.rules; then
    echo "$IMMUTABLE_RULE" > "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"/immutable.rules
fi

if ! grep -q "immutable.rules" /etc/audit/audit.rules; then
    echo "-i $AUDIT_RULES_DIR/immutable.rules" >> /etc/audit/audit.rules
fi

# Restart the auditd service to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd

# Verify the audit rules are loaded and immutable
auditctl -l | grep -q "$IMMUTABLE_RULE" || echo "Failed to set audit rules as immutable"

audit_rule_64="-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S mount -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k export"

add_audit_rule "$audit_rule_64"

augenrules --load

Write an idempotent bash remediation script that ensures the system audit daemon generates audit records for all write and attribute changes to that sudo configuration directory, tagged so the events are identifiable, and that the rule persists across reboots and applies regardless of whether the daemon loads rules via the generation utility or directly.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule
AUDIT_RULE="-w /etc/sudoers.d -p wa -k sudo_privilege_mod"

# Check if the rule is already present
if ! auditctl -l | grep -q -- "$AUDIT_RULE"; then
    # Add the rule to the audit system
    auditctl -a always,exit -F dir=/etc/sudoers.d -F perm=wa -k sudo_privilege_mod
fi

# Ensure the rule is persistent across reboots
if ! grep -q -- "$AUDIT_RULE" /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules; then
    echo "$AUDIT_RULE" >> /etc/audit/rules.d/audit.rules
fi

# Reload audit rules to ensure they are applied
augenrules --load
```

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, configure the system audit subsystem to generate an audit record whenever a user executes a program in a way that elevates their effective privileges — specifically whenever a process is executed with an effective user or group ID that differs from the real one (i.e. privilege escalation via setuid/setgid execution). The configuration must cover both 32-bit and 64-bit system architectures and apply to both the user-ID and group-ID escalation cases. These audit records help detect misuse of privileged functions by insiders or compromised accounts. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that establishes these audit rules so they persist across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rules for privilege escalation
audit_rules=(
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C uid!=euid -F key=privileged"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S execve -C gid!=egid -F key=privileged"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C uid!=euid -F key=privileged"
    "-a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S execve -C gid!=egid -F key=privileged"
)

# File where audit rules are stored
audit_rules_file="/etc/audit/rules.d/privilege-escalation.rules"

# Ensure the audit rules file exists
touch "$audit_rules_file"

# Function to add a rule if it doesn't already exist
add_audit_rule() {
    local rule="$1"
    if ! grep -Fxq "$rule" "$audit_rules_file"; then
        echo "$rule" >> "$audit_rules_file"
    fi
}

# Add each audit rule
for rule in "${audit_rules[@]}"; do
    add_audit_rule "$rule"
done

# Restart the audit daemon to apply changes
service auditd restart
```

The audit rule must persist across reboots and be loaded regardless of which rule-loading mechanism the audit daemon uses, and the configuration must be idempotent. Write a bash remediation script that achieves this.

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule
AUDIT_RULE="-w /etc/group -p wa -k group_changes"

# Create the audit rules directory if it doesn't exist
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit/rules.d"
mkdir -p "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"

# Define the audit rules file
AUDIT_RULES_FILE="$AUDIT_RULES_DIR/group_changes.rules"

# Ensure the audit rule is present in the rules file

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit rule
AUDIT_RULE="-w /etc/gshadow -p wa -k audit_group_mod"

    auditctl -l | grep -q -- "$AUDIT_RULE"

RULE_32="-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S fchmodat -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -F key=perm_mod"

    fi

  "-a always,exit -F arch=b32 -S rename,renameat,renameat2 -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k file-rename"

#!/bin/bash

AUDIT_RULE="-a always,exit -F path=/usr/lib/openssh/ssh-keysign -F perm=x -F auid>=1000 -F auid!=unset -k privileged"

check_rule() {

Acceptable compliant responses for such errors are limited to switching to single-user mode, halting the system, or notifying via the system logger; your configuration must select the single-user-mode response. Write an idempotent bash remediation script that enforces this behavior and persists across reboots.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the audit daemon configuration file exists
AUDIT_CONF="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
if [ ! -f "$AUDIT_CONF" ]; then
    echo "Audit configuration file not found. Exiting."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the current configuration
cp "$AUDIT_CONF" "${AUDIT_CONF}.bak"

# Set the action to take when a disk error occurs
sed -i '/^disk_error_action/d' "$AUDIT_CONF"
echo "disk_error_action = single" >> "$AUDIT_CONF"

# Ensure the audit daemon service is enabled and started
systemctl enable auditd
systemctl restart auditd
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the auditd package is installed
if ! dpkg -l | grep -q auditd; then
    apt-get update
    apt-get install -y auditd

# Configure auditd to switch to single-user mode on disk full

grep -q "^action_mail_acct = root" "$AUDIT_CONF" || echo "action_mail_acct = root" >> "$AUDIT_CONF"
grep -q "^admin_space_left_action = single" "$AUDIT_CONF" || echo "admin_space_left_action = single" >> "$AUDIT_CONF"

# Restart auditd to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd

# Ensure auditd starts on boot
systemctl enable auditd

response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Define variables
AUDITD_CONF="/etc/audit/auditd.conf"
EMAIL_RECIPIENTS="admin@example.com"
SPACE_LEFT_ACTION="exec /usr/local/bin/auditd_low_space_alert.sh"

# Create the alert script
cat << 'EOF' > /usr/local/bin/auditd_low_space_alert.sh
#!/bin/bash
echo "Audit daemon low disk space warning on $(hostname)" | mail -s "Auditd Low Disk Space Alert" admin@example.com
EOF

# Make the alert script executable
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/auditd_low_space_alert.sh

# Backup the original auditd.conf if not already backed up
if [ ! -f "${AUDITD_CONF}.bak" ]; then
    cp "${AUDITD_CONF}" "${AUDITD_CONF}.bak"
fi

# Update auditd.conf to set the space_left_action
if grep -q "^space_left_action" "${AUDITD_CONF}"; then
    sed -i "s|^space_left_action.*|space_left_action = ${SPACE_LEFT_ACTION}|" "${AUDITD_CONF}"
else
    echo "space_left_action = ${SPACE_LEFT_ACTION}" >> "${AUDITD_CONF}"
fi

# Restart auditd to apply changes
systemctl restart auditd
```

prompt:
On an Ubuntu 24.04 system, the rule files that define which events the system audit daemon records must be protected from unauthorized reading or modification, since loose permissions could let unprivileged users discover or tamper with audit coverage and hide malicious activity. Ensure that every audit rule definition file used by the audit subsystem permits access only to its owner (read and write, with no permissions for group or others, i.e. mode 0600). Write an idempotent bash remediation script that brings these files into compliance.

Respond with ONLY a single self-contained bash script and nothing else. Enclose the entire script in one fenced code block that starts with a line containing exactly ```bash and ends with a line containing exactly ```. Do not write any explanation, comments, or text before or after the code block.
response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Find all audit rule files
audit_rule_files=$(find /etc/audit /etc/audit/rules.d -type f -name '*.rules')

# Set permissions to 0600 for each file found
for file in $audit_rule_files; do
    chmod 0600 "$file"
done
```
